content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED
IN PART
by Reg.18945
(see A-98-072)

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 25, 1991

Kathryn E. Donovan
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 335
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Confirmation of
Telephone Advice
Our File No. I-91-357

Dear Ms. Donovan:

This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice concerning the application of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act")1 to sources of gifts to public officials.

Since your request does not identify a specific person to
whom the advice will apply, we treat it as a request for informal
advice pursuant to Regulation 18329(b)(2)(A).2

In our conversation, you presented five different factual
situations involving donations to a nonprofit charitable
organization and inquired whether the donating entity would be a
"source" of the gift where the nonprofit charity used the
donations to make a gift to public officials. The factual
situations you presented were as follows:

(1) A business entity makes an annual dues payment or
similar unrestricted donation to a nonprofit charitable
organization. The charitable organization uses money from its
general fund to pay for fundraising events. For the first time in

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-357
Page 2


the history of the charitable organization's existence, the
organization invites, among others, one or more California public
officials to attend the fundraising event free of charge. One or
more of those officials accepts the invitation and actually
attends the event. The business entity has no direction or
control over the decision to provide free admission to public
officials or the decision to invite any particular officials.

     (2) A business entity makes an annual dues payment or
similar unrestricted donation to a nonprofit charitable
organization. The charitable organization uses money from its
general fund to pay for fundraising events. In 1991, as in prior
years, the charitable organization invites, among others, one or
more California public officials to attend the fundraising event
free of charge. One or more of those public officials accepts the
invitation and actually attends the event. The business entity
has no direction or control over the decision to provide free
admission to public officials or the decision to invite any
particular officials.

     (3) A business entity makes a donation to a nonprofit
charitable organization. The business entity designates its
donation for the charitable organization to conduct a specific
fundraising event. The charitable organization invites, among
others, a group of public officials to attend the fundraising
event. One or more of those public officials accepts the
invitation and actually attend the event. At the time the
business entity makes the donation to the charitable organization,
the business entity knows that public officials will be invited to
attend the fundraising event free of charge. However, the
business entity does not direct or control the decision to invite
any particular official, nor does it know at the time it makes the
donation to the charitable organization that any particular
official actually will attend the event.

     (4) A business entity makes a donation to a nonprofit
charitable organization. The business entity designates its
donation for the charitable organization to conduct a specific
fundraising event. The charitable organization invites, among
others, a group of public officials to attend the fundraising
event. One or more of those public officials accepts the
invitation and actually attends the event. At the time the
business entity makes the donation to the charitable organization,
the organization has informed the business entity that one or more
specific public officials will attend the event free of charge,
but the business entity had no direction or control over the
decision to invite any particular public officials.

     (5) A business entity makes a donation to a nonprofit
charitable organization. The business entity designates its
donation for the charitable organization to conduct a specific
fundraising event and provides the organization with a list of
invitees, including various public officials.

Our File No. I-91-357
Page 3


     This is to confirm that I advised you that in each of the
situations described the public officials who attend the
fundraising event for a charitable organization have received a gift. The value
of the gift is the cost of the portion of the event ticket that
exceeds the charitable donation portion actually stated on the
ticket face or, if no donation amount is stated, the fair market
value of the food, beverage or other tangible benefits provided to
the attendees.  (See Section 82028 and Regulation 18726.8(a).)

     Also, I advised you that, in situations (1), (2) and (3), the
charitable organization is the source of the gift.  However,
please note that in situation (3) at the time the donation is made, does not know
the identity of the public officials who will be invited to attend
the fundraising event.  If the business entity does know the
identity at the time of making the donation the situation appears
to be similar to situation (4).  In situation (4), I advised that
it is likely that the business entity is the source of the gift
and, in situation (5), the business entity is clearly the source
of the gift.  Where, as in situations (4) and (5), the business
entity is the source of the gift, the charitable organization
would be an intermediary for the making of the gift pursuant to
Section 87210.

     Please note that, since our telephone conversation, the
Commission staff has drafted proposed regulations which, if
adopted by the Commission, will bear upon the issues addressed in
this letter.  Specifically, proposed Regulation 18945(a) offers
the Commission several options to select in defining when a person
is the source of a gift to a public official.  Furthermore, the
proposed amendments to Regulation 18726.8 (to be renumbered as
Regulation 18946.4) offer the Commission the option to determine
that tickets to charitable fundraisers have no value in certain
situations.  Finally, please note that proposed Regulation 18945.3
defines who is an intermediary for the making of gifts.  We expect
the Commission to discuss these regulations in detail at its
January 1992 meeting and adopt some version of the regulations in
the spring of 1991.

     Should you have any questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

SH:aa

sections.question: In five different factual situations involving donations to a nonprofit charitable organization, whether the donating entity would be a 'source' of the gift where the nonprofit charity used the donations to make a gift to public officials. The five scenarios varied by: (1) unrestricted annual dues with no donor control over public official invitations; (2) unrestricted annual dues in ongoing pattern with no donor control; (3) designated donation for specific event knowing public officials will be invited but not knowing which specific ones will attend; (4) designated donation where organization informed donor that specific public officials will attend; (5) designated donation with donor providing the list of public official invitees.
sections.conclusion: I advised you that in each of the situations described the public officials who attend the fundraising event free of charge have received a gift. The value of the gift is the cost of the portion of the event ticket that exceeds the charitable donation portion actually stated on the ticket face or, if no donation amount is stated, the fair market value of the food, beverage or other tangible benefits provided to the attendees. In situations (1), (2) and (3), the charitable organization is the source of the gift. In situation (4), it is likely that the business entity is the source of the gift and the charitable organization would be an intermediary for the making of the gift. In situation (5), the business entity is clearly the source of the gift.
sections.question_synthetic: Under the Political Reform Act, when a business entity donates to a nonprofit charitable organization that subsequently uses those funds to provide gifts (free admission to events) to public officials, is the business entity a 'source' of the gift to the public officials, and does the answer depend on the degree of donor control, knowledge, and designation?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Public officials attending such events receive taxable gifts valued at the fair market value of benefits received. The charitable organization is the gift source when donations are unrestricted or the donor lacks knowledge of specific officials who will attend (situations 1-3), but the business entity becomes the likely or clear source when it designates the donation for a specific event where it knows or specifies which officials will attend (situations 4-5).
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document confirms prior telephone advice per Regulation 18329(b)(2)(A). Contains specific factual scenarios 1-5 and corresponding conclusions. Notes that proposed regulations were being drafted to address these issues. Date appears to be November 25, 1991. Minor OCR artifacts present but core content is clear and extractable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In five different factual situations involving donations to a nonprofit charitable organization, whether the donating entity would be a 'source' of the gift where the nonprofit charity used the donations to make a gift to public officials. The five scenarios varied by: (1) unrestricted annual dues with no donor control over public official invitations; (2) unrestricted annual dues in ongoing pattern with no donor control; (3) designated donation for specific event knowing public officials will be invited but not knowing which specific ones will attend; (4) designated donation where organization informed donor that specific public officials will attend; (5) designated donation with donor providing the list of public official invitees.

CONCLUSION: I advised you that in each of the situations described the public officials who attend the fundraising event free of charge have received a gift. The value of the gift is the cost of the portion of the event ticket that exceeds the charitable donation portion actually stated on the ticket face or, if no donation amount is stated, the fair market value of the food, beverage or other tangible benefits provided to the attendees. In situations (1), (2) and (3), the charitable organization is the source of the gift. In situation (4), it is likely that the business entity is the source of the gift and the charitable organization would be an intermediary for the making of the gift. In situation (5), the business entity is clearly the source of the gift.

embedding.summary: This informal advice letter confirms telephone advice regarding when a business entity making donations to a nonprofit charitable organization that uses those funds to provide free event admission to public officials is considered the 'source' of a gift under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC's position depends on the degree of donor knowledge, control, and designation, with the charitable organization being the source in unrestricted or low-control scenarios and the business entity being the source in scenarios with specific designation or knowledge of which officials will attend.
